flashcards.front: What is the purpose of MPI_Barrier in timing parallel programs?
flashcards.back: MPI_Barrier synchronizes all processes to ensure they reach the same execution point before timing begins and ends, providing accurate parallel execution measurements.

flashcards.front: Which process typically handles timing in MPI programs and why?
flashcards.back: Rank 0 (master process) typically handles timing to avoid clock synchronization issues and provide a consistent measurement reference across all processes.

flashcards.front: What does MPI_Wtime() return and what is its precision?
flashcards.back: MPI_Wtime() returns a double-precision floating-point number representing elapsed seconds since an arbitrary time in the past, with high precision suitable for performance measurements.

flashcards.front: Why is the two-barrier approach used in MPI timing?
flashcards.back: The first barrier ensures all processes are ready before timing starts; the second ensures all processes complete before timing ends, capturing the full parallel execution time.

flashcards.front: What type of time does the difference between MPI_Wtime() calls measure?
flashcards.back: It measures wall-clock time (elapsed real time) rather than CPU time, capturing the actual time taken for parallel execution including communication overhead.

flashcards.front: Why is wall-clock time preferred over CPU time for parallel programs?
flashcards.back: Wall-clock time includes all overhead: communication delays, synchronization waits, and idle time due to load imbalance. CPU time only measures active computation and misses these parallel costs.

flashcards.front: What happens without the first MPI_Barrier before timing?
flashcards.back: Fast processes start timing early while slow ones are still initializing, giving inaccurate and inconsistent start times across processes.

flashcards.front: What happens without the second MPI_Barrier after parallel work?
flashcards.back: The master process may stop timing before all worker processes have finished their computation, leading to incomplete and misleadingly short timing measurements.

flashcards.front: How can MPI_Reduce be used in timing analysis?
flashcards.back: MPI_Reduce with MPI_MAX can aggregate local execution times from all processes, finding the maximum time which represents the true parallel execution time (determined by the slowest process).

flashcards.front: What are best practices for timing MPI programs?
flashcards.back: Run multiple trials (report average/minimum), use MPI_Wtime for high-resolution timing, time only the parallel section, separate initialization from computation, and account for timer overhead on short runs.
